---
title: "From LLM Interaction Patterns to VS Code Chat Modes"
subtitle: "Ask, Edit, Agent, and Plan through the lens of tools"
date: 2026-03-19
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    width: 1400
    height: 900
    margin: 0.04
    controls: true
    progress: true
    embed-resources: true
    css: styles.css
---

## Three interaction patterns

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Most LLM products fit one of three interaction patterns.

- Plain chat: one user turn in, one model turn out.
- Chat with tools: the model may pause to call something like search.
- Full agentic loop: the model can search, act, inspect, and keep going until it reaches a stop condition.

The practical difference is mostly the tool budget, and how much control you hand to the system between turns.
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/interaction-patterns.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Plain chat grows context

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Plain chat is still a loop, but it is a turn-based one.

- Each new turn appends to the same conversation history.
- Later responses are shaped by the earlier turns that remain in context.
- The model can explain, compare, or draft, but it does not fetch new evidence on its own.

This is the simplest mental model: the thread grows, and the next answer depends on that growing thread.
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/chat-thread.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Chat With Tools: search example

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Tool use turns one model response into a small internal loop.

- The model emits a tool call such as `search(...)`.
- The search results come back as structured tool output.
- Those results are appended to the conversation, then the model continues with better evidence.

The key difference is not a new kind of model. It is the extra tool-result message inserted into context.
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/chat-search.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Full agentic loop adds iteration

::: {.callout}
This is where "chat with tools" becomes a real agent loop:
each `tool_result` is appended to `messages[]`, then the model is called again until the stop reason changes.
:::

::: {.full-loop-figure}
![](assets/agent-loop-s01.svg)
:::

::: {.source-note}
Adapted from [learn-claude-code](https://github.com/shareAI-lab/learn-claude-code) and the interactive [s01 The Agent Loop](https://learn.shareai.run/en/s01/), then recreated locally for reliable RevealJS rendering.
:::

## What changes from mode to mode?

::: {.hero}
The core difference is not "how smart Copilot is."

It is which tools the model can use, and how much freedom it has to use them.
:::

::: {.source-note}
Deck built from official VS Code and GitHub Copilot documentation current on March 9, 2026, reviewed on March 19, 2026.
:::

## The picker is really a tool picker

::: {.mode-cards}
::: {.mode-card}
**Ask**

Read, search, explain, suggest.
:::

::: {.mode-card}
**Edit**

Controlled edits in a user-chosen working set.
:::

::: {.mode-card}
**Agent**

Search, edit, run commands, iterate, and use external tools.
:::

::: {.mode-card}
**Plan**

Research first, then produce a reviewed implementation plan.
:::
:::

::: {.callout}
For an intro audience, this is the clean mental model:
Ask is answer-first, Edit is bounded editing, Agent is autonomous action, and Plan is read-only planning before action.
:::

::: {.source-note}
VS Code now treats Edit as deprecated, so the three modern working styles are mainly Ask, Agent, and Plan. Edit still appears in older tutorials and some current IDE flows.
:::

## Compare the tool budgets

::: {.matrix-wrap}
| Default posture | Ask | Edit | Agent | Plan |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Search and read the codebase | Yes. Agentic search and context gathering | Limited. Finds relevant context once | Yes. Repeats searches as needed | Yes. Read-only research |
| Write files | No direct writes. You apply suggestions | Yes, but in the files you chose | Yes. It can choose files and edit them | No |
| Run terminal commands or tests | Not the normal loop | No | Yes | No by default |
| Use MCP or extension tools | Sometimes through tool mentions, but not the main posture | Not the point | Yes | Optional extra tools, but planning starts read-only |
| Who decides the steps? | Mostly you | You choose files and steer each turn | The agent decides and iterates | The plan agent breaks the work down |
| Main output | Explanation, options, code blocks | Proposed edits for review | Completed task progress plus edits and tool runs | A plan, checks, and open questions |
:::

::: {.source-note}
Intro simplification: this table shows each mode's default posture, not every edge case.
:::

## Ask Mode

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Ask mode is best when you want understanding before action.

- It automatically searches your workspace and gathers context.
- It is good for explanations, options, and code suggestions.
- The loop usually stops at "answer" rather than "act."
- You stay responsible for applying any code changes.

Use Ask for prompts like:

- "Where is authentication configured in this project?"
- "Show me two ways to refactor this parser."
- "Explain this error in plain English."
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/ask-loop.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Edit Mode

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Edit mode is the narrowest editing mode.

- It is useful when you already know the files you want to touch.
- You choose the working set, then Copilot proposes edits there.
- It does not run the wider autonomous search-and-fix loop.
- Official docs mark it as deprecated in favor of Ask or Agent.

Use Edit for prompts like:

- "Rename these props in the two open components."
- "Update this test file and its fixture, but nothing else."
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/edit-loop.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Agent Mode

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Agent mode is the full action loop.

- It can search, edit, run commands, inspect problems, and iterate.
- It is the mode built for multi-step work across several files.
- It can also use MCP and extension-provided tools when enabled.
- You still review approvals and results, but the agent does the legwork.

Use Agent for prompts like:

- "Implement OAuth login and wire the callback route."
- "Fix the failing tests and update the snapshots."
- "Refactor this feature and run the test suite."
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/agent-loop.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Plan Mode

::: {.mode-layout}
::: {.mode-copy}
Plan mode is a research-and-breakdown loop.

- It uses read-only analysis first.
- It asks clarifying questions when the task is ambiguous.
- It produces implementation steps, verification ideas, and open questions.
- No code changes happen until you approve a handoff to implementation.

Use Plan for prompts like:

- "Plan a migration from REST calls to GraphQL."
- "Break this feature into implementation and verification steps."
- "Map the risks before we touch the billing flow."
:::

::: {.mode-figure}
![](assets/plan-loop.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

## Fast chooser

::: {.mode-cards}
::: {.mode-card}
**Pick Ask when...**

you want explanations, options, or a fast read on the codebase.
:::

::: {.mode-card}
**Pick Edit when...**

you want tightly bounded edits in files you already selected.
:::

::: {.mode-card}
**Pick Agent when...**

you want the tool-using loop to keep going until the task is done.
:::

::: {.mode-card}
**Pick Plan when...**

you want a safe roadmap before any code changes happen.
:::
:::

::: {.source-note}
Sources:
[learn-claude-code repository](https://github.com/shareAI-lab/learn-claude-code),
[learn-claude-code interactive s01](https://learn.shareai.run/en/s01/),
[VS Code workspace context](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/reference/workspace-context),
[VS Code tools](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/concepts/tools),
[VS Code local agents](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/agents/local-agents),
[VS Code planning](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/agents/planning),
[VS Code Copilot feature cheat sheet](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/copilot/reference/copilot-vscode-features),
[GitHub Docs chat in IDE](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/chat-with-copilot/chat-in-ide)
:::
